items.title: Show HN: Handoff – a verified context bridge between Claude Code sessions
items.why_it_matters: Handoff is a new open-source tool designed to bridge context between separate Claude Code sessions. If you frequently lose momentum or have to re-explain your project structure to Claude when restarting your terminal, this tool packages your current session state so the next run starts with full context. You can install this skill in your local Claude Code CLI today to seamlessly resume building your AI-ingest app without repeating yourself.

items.title: Show HN: Self-healing review gate and knowledge base for Claude Code (Beta)
items.why_it_matters: Verity.md is a self-healing review gate built specifically for AI-driven development workflows. It acts as an adversarial review layer that checks your agent's code for intent gaps and security issues, then feeds instructions back to the agent to fix itself. Since you want to get better at verifying what your AI writes, you can connect your AI-ingest GitHub repo to their beta this week to automate your code reviews.

items.title: Claude Code cache guard because Fable 5 tokens are way too precious to waste
items.why_it_matters: Claude Cache Guard is a utility designed to prevent Claude Code from wasting expensive prompt tokens by managing how and when context is cached. If you are vibe coding on a budget, this tool helps ensure you aren't paying repeatedly for the same codebase context during long sessions. You can clone this repo and add the guard to your local Claude Code configuration today to keep your API costs down while building.

items.title: Show HN: Peek-CLI: Let Claude Code See the Browser
items.why_it_matters: Peek-CLI is a lightweight command-line tool that allows Claude Code to 'see' your local browser window. Instead of manually copying HTML or taking screenshots to debug layout issues, this tool lets your terminal agent inspect your live frontend directly. You can install Peek-CLI today and use it to let Claude Code debug a CSS or layout bug on your local Next.js dev server.

items.title: Show HN: Heckle – Send a bug's full browser context to your coding agent
items.why_it_matters: Heckle is an open-source tool that captures a bug's full browser context—including console logs and DOM state—and packages it directly for your coding agent. This eliminates the tedious back-and-forth of explaining frontend errors to Claude. You can install Heckle this week to capture your next Next.js runtime error and feed it straight to Claude Code for an instant fix.

items.title: Show HN: Sidenote – comment on your rendered blog, an LLM writes the Git diff
items.why_it_matters: Sidenote lets you comment directly on your rendered local website and automatically generates a Git diff to apply those changes. It bridges the gap between visual design tweaks and code updates, making it perfect for rapid frontend iteration. You can install Sidenote on your local Next.js dev server today to visually edit your AI-ingest UI and let the AI handle the Git commit.

items.title: SigMap: 97% token reduction for AI coding sessions
items.why_it_matters: SigMap is a tool that claims a 97% token reduction for AI coding sessions by optimizing how codebase context is mapped and sent to LLMs. If you are running into context limits or high bills while using Claude Code on your project, this tool slashes the noise in your prompts. You can run SigMap against your AI-ingest repository directory today to see how much context window you can save before your next coding session.

items.title: Show HN: An unmetered LLM API–$6/month, no token tracking, no limits
items.why_it_matters: Yolo-auto has launched an unmetered LLM API for $6/month using the Qwen3.6-35B model with a 128k context window and no token limits. While you should save Claude for complex reasoning, this is perfect for high-volume, repetitive tasks like background data processing. You can swap your AI-ingest backend's summarization API key to this endpoint this week to run unlimited article filtering without worrying about your budget.

items.title: sqlite-utils 4.0rc2, mostly written by Claude Fable (for about $149.25)
items.why_it_matters: Developer Simon Willison successfully shipped the sqlite-utils 4.0 release candidate, with the majority of the code written by Claude Fable for a total cost of $149.25. This is a real-world validation of using AI to drive major open-source updates on a tight budget. You can use Claude Code to refactor or upgrade a major dependency in your own Next.js project this week to practice managing a large AI-driven codebase update.

items.title: Claude Design System Prompt
items.why_it_matters: The Claude Design System Prompt is a highly optimized system prompt designed to help Claude generate clean, consistent, and modern UI components. If you struggle with making your Next.js frontends look polished, this prompt forces the AI to adhere to strict design principles. You can paste this prompt into your next Claude session this week to generate a clean, responsive layout for your AI-ingest dashboard.

items.title: Show HN: Contextrot– Analyze where Claude Code degrades from its own session log
items.why_it_matters: Contextrot is an open-source tool that analyzes your Claude Code session logs to identify exactly where and why the model's performance starts to degrade. This helps you understand when to clear your terminal context before the AI starts writing buggy code. You can run Contextrot on your last long AI-ingest development log today to find the exact moment Claude lost track of your project.
